document.title: SIMPLE GUIDE: Page Index vs RAG Context Retrieval Approaches
document.description: This technical guide compares two primary retrieval strategies for LLM context: Page Indexing and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). Page Indexing is a deterministic, structure-based approach ideal for small datasets (< few hundred pages) and slide generation, while RAG utilizes semantic similarity via vector embeddings and databases like Pinecone or FAISS for large-scale, unstructured enterprise data. The document details step-by-step workflows, infrastructure requirements, use-case suitability, and provides a comparative analysis of pros, cons, and scalability trade-offs.

nodes.title: Introduction and Definition of Page Index Retrieval
nodes.summary: Introduces Page Indexing as a retrieval method based on document structure rather than semantic similarity. It is described as a deterministic process where the system searches a page index (e.g., Page 1 for Intro, Page 2 for Architecture), finds the relevant page, and retrieves content to be broken down slide-by-slide or section-by-section for LLM processing.

nodes.title: How Page Index Works
nodes.summary: Outlines a 4-step workflow for Page Indexing: 1) Document Parsing to extract headings and build the index; 2) User Query processing (e.g., 'Explain embeddings'); 3) Index Search to match keywords to specific pages (e.g., Page 4); and 4) Content Processing to segment retrieved data for the LLM.

nodes.title: What is RAG?
nodes.summary: Defines Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) as a semantic similarity search method using vector embeddings instead of page numbers. The pipeline involves chunking documents, generating embeddings, and utilizing a Vector DB to find meaningfully related content rather than exact keyword matches.

nodes.title: How RAG Works
nodes.summary: Details the 5-step RAG pipeline: 1) Chunking text; 2) Generating Embeddings (e.g., vectors like [0.23, 0.89...]); 3) Storage in Vector Databases such as Pinecone, Weaviate, FAISS, or Chroma; 4) Query Retrieval via similarity search; and 5) LLM Generation using retrieved chunks as context.

nodes.title: Key Differences Table
nodes.summary: Provides a direct comparison between Page Index and RAG. Page Index relies on keyword/page lookup, document index storage, and light infrastructure, but has limited scalability. RAG utilizes semantic similarity and vector databases, offering superior matching and high scalability at the cost of a complex embedding pipeline.

nodes.title: When to Use Page Index
nodes.summary: Identifies optimal use cases for Page Indexing: slide generators, educational content, static manuals, and small datasets (< few hundred pages). Benefits include high speed, zero embedding costs, simple architecture, and easy debugging.

nodes.title: When to Use RAG
nodes.summary: Specifies RAG use cases: enterprise assistants, customer support chatbots, large document collections (millions of docs), and unstructured data like emails or reports. Benefits include semantic search, handling ambiguous questions, and high flexibility.

nodes.title: Pros and Cons Analysis
nodes.summary: Contrasts the advantages and disadvantages of both systems. Page Index is fast and cheap but structure-dependent and poor for large data. RAG handles vague questions and large datasets but incurs embedding costs and requires infrastructure tuning.

nodes.title: Simple Analogy: Textbook vs. Librarian
nodes.summary: Uses a conceptual analogy to explain retrieval: Page Index is like a textbook index where you go directly to Page 42; RAG is like a 'Smart Librarian' who finds and synthesizes relevant paragraphs across multiple books to answer a query.

nodes.title: Quick Summary and Recommendations
nodes.summary: Concludes with a final recommendation: use Page Index for small structured documents, tutorials, and slide generation; use RAG for chatbots, large enterprise data, and semantic search. Encourages selection based on data size and structure.
